manifest.hypothesis: Vision Transformers (ViTs) encode concepts with higher semantic diversity but lower spatial precision at intermediate layers compared to CNNs, leading to measurable differences in transfer learning performance for fine-grained classification tasks. | ViTs will exhibit 15-25% higher semantic diversity in intermediate layers (e.g., layers 4-8) but 10-20% lower spatial precision compared to CNNs, as measured by neuron labeling and Grad-CAM localization error. This trade-off will correlate with a 5-10% drop in transfer learning accuracy for fine-grained tasks (e.g., CUB-200) when using intermediate-layer features from ViTs vs. CNNs. | Train ViT-B/16 and ResNet-50 on ImageNet-1K, then freeze intermediate layers and fine-tune on fine-grained datasets. Use neuron labeling (Paper 1) to quantify concept diversity and Grad-CAM to measure spatial precision. Compare transfer learning performance across layers. | Semantic diversity (concept count per layer), spatial precision (Grad-CAM localization error in pixels), transfer learning accuracy (%) on CUB-200 and iNaturalist datasets

manifest.hypothesis: Applying differential privacy with adaptive noise scaling to federated learning for 3D medical imaging (e.g., MRI segmentation) will preserve model utility (Dice score) within 5% of non-private baselines while reducing the success rate of membership inference attacks by at least 30% compared to fixed-noise DP. | Adaptive noise scaling will maintain Dice scores within 5% of non-private FL models while reducing membership inference attack success rates by ≥30% for ε ≤ 5. | FL setup with 10 clients, each holding 3D MRI scans from the BraTS dataset. Train a U-Net model for tumor segmentation using FedAvg with DP-SGD. Compare adaptive noise (scaled by gradient magnitude) vs. fixed noise across ε values. | Dice score for segmentation accuracy; attack success rate (ASR) for membership inference attacks.

manifest.hypothesis: Applying differential privacy with adaptive noise scaling to federated learning for 3D medical imaging (e.g., MRI or CT scans) will preserve diagnostic utility (measured by Dice similarity coefficient) while maintaining a privacy budget (ε ≤ 5) comparable to 2D imaging tasks. | Adaptive noise scaling will achieve a 10-15% higher Dice similarity coefficient than fixed noise scaling for 3D images, with ε ≤ 5 in both cases. | Train a federated U-Net model on a distributed dataset of 3D MRI scans (e.g., BraTS or private clinical data) with adaptive and fixed DP noise. Compare segmentation performance and privacy budget across conditions. | Dice similarity coefficient for segmentation tasks, privacy budget (ε) for differential privacy.

manifest.hypothesis: Assuming 'w' represents neural network weights, initializing 'w' using a hybrid distribution (e.g., uniform for small values and Gaussian for large values) will reduce training time by 15-20% compared to standard Gaussian initialization, without degrading final model accuracy (uncertain due to lack of prior evidence). | Hybrid initialization will achieve faster convergence (15-20% fewer epochs) while maintaining or improving accuracy within ±1% of baseline. | Train ResNet-18 and Transformer models on CIFAR-10 and IMDB datasets with three initialization strategies. Use identical hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, optimizer) across all runs. | Number of epochs to reach 95% of baseline accuracy; final test accuracy (%)

manifest.hypothesis: Heterogeneous ensembles (e.g., combining CNNs, Transformers, and Gradient Boosting models) will outperform homogeneous ensembles (e.g., multiple instances of the same model architecture) on high-dimensional, sparse datasets, as measured by classification accuracy and F1-score, due to increased model diversity capturing complementary feature representations. | Heterogeneous ensembles will achieve at least 5% higher accuracy and F1-score compared to homogeneous ensembles on high-dimensional datasets (e.g., genomics or NLP embeddings). | Train and evaluate homogeneous and heterogeneous ensembles on benchmark high-dimensional datasets (e.g., TCGA for genomics, GLUE for NLP). Use cross-validation and hold-out test sets to measure performance. Include adversarial noise to test robustness. | Accuracy, F1-score, and area under the ROC curve (AUC).

manifest.hypothesis: Heterogeneous ensemble models (e.g., combining logistic regression, random forest, and gradient boosting) will outperform homogeneous ensembles (e.g., multiple random forests) in credit scoring tasks when trained on class-imbalanced datasets, as measured by AUC-ROC and F1-score. | Heterogeneous ensembles will achieve at least 3-5% higher AUC-ROC and F1-score compared to homogeneous ensembles under severe class imbalance (e.g., 99:1). | Train and evaluate homogeneous (e.g., 5x random forest) and heterogeneous (e.g., logistic regression + random forest + XGBoost) ensembles on public credit scoring datasets (e.g., German Credit Data, Home Equity Line of Credit (HELOC)) with synthetically induced class imbalance. Use 5-fold cross-validation and repeat experiments 10 times for statistical significance. | AUC-ROC, F1-score, precision-recall curves.
